query.sentence:
Beyond PEEK, which thermoplastic materials can:
- Withstand continuous or intermittent use up to 200 °C
- Offer good chemical compatibility with acid, bases, organic solvents, oils
- Be co extruded with softer polymers such as TPU, TPE, or TPV (e.g., in multilayer or jacketed constructions)
query.application_Y: Withstand 200°C, good chemical compatibility with acid, bases, organic solvents, oils, co extrusion with softer polymers

properties: continuous operating temperature ≥200 °C (or intermittent up to 200 °C)
excellent chemical resistance to strong acids, bases, organic solvents and oils
low moisture absorption (<0.1 %) and dimensional stability at high temperatures
sufficient melt strength for co‑extrusion with TPU/TPE/TPV layers
compatible melt flow index (MFI) range 5–20 g/10 min @230 °C to allow twin‑screw extrusion
good mechanical toughness and modulus comparable to PEEK (tensile >70 MPa, elongation >5 %)
constraints: melt viscosity must not exceed the capacity of a standard 2–3 mm screw extruder used in lab scale
processing temperature window should be ≤300 °C to avoid degradation of co‑extruded elastomeric layers
compatibilizer (e.g., maleic anhydride grafted polyolefin) required for interfacial adhesion with TPU/TPE/TPV
material must be available in a grade that can be melt‑blended without pre‑treatment (no cross‑linking or curing step)
cost and supply chain should allow lab‑scale procurement of at least 10 kg per batch

final_candidate.justification: DIC.PPS has a melting point of ~280 °C and a heat deflection temperature above 215 °C, satisfying the ≥200 °C requirement. PPS is well known for its outstanding chemical resistance to acids, bases, organic solvents and oils (see materialdb Doc 5). It can be processed as a melt‑flowable thermoplastic in twin‑screw extruders, enabling co‑extrusion with TPU/TPE/TPV when combined with a maleic anhydride grafted polyolefin compatibilizer. The linear cross‑linked structure provides dimensional stability and mechanical toughness comparable to PEEK while being less expensive and more readily available for lab‑scale production.

process_recipe.description: Dry blend the PPS pellets (DIC.PPS) with a maleic anhydride grafted polypropylene compatibilizer (MAPP, 2 wt%) and TPU pellets (TPU‑A, 10 mm diameter).
process_recipe.conditions: Blend in a tumble mixer for 5 min at room temperature.

process_recipe.description: Feed the blend into a twin‑screw extruder equipped with a temperature profile: 260 °C (feed zone), 280 °C (mid‑zone), 270 °C (die).
process_recipe.conditions: Screw speed 50–70 rpm, barrel pressure ~10 bar.
process_recipe.equipment_class: Lab‑scale twin‑screw extruder (e.g., Brabender or Thermo‑Scientific)

process_recipe.description: Co‑extrude a second TPU layer by feeding TPU pellets into the same extruder downstream of the PPS melt, using a two‑channel die that merges the streams at ~190 °C for TPU.
process_recipe.conditions: Maintain TPU channel temperature 190–200 °C; adjust screw speed to keep overall residence time <5 s.

process_recipe.description: Collect the multilayer film on a chilled roller, then perform a post‑heat treatment at 260 °C for 30 s to ensure interlayer adhesion.
Cut the film into test specimens for mechanical, thermal, and chemical resistance testing.
